methodology_long: Two numbers per team. POWER SCORE is current strength: each team is scored across the fourteen active categories on a recency-weighted estimate (exponential decay, λ=0.85, so the most recent week is ~17% of the estimate and the preseason projection is ignored entirely), 12 points for leading a category down to 1 for last. That ordering is the rank. PLAYOFF ODDS is a separate, schedule-aware projection: a 40,000-run Monte Carlo of the remaining regular season (Weeks 15-22). Every category in every remaining matchup is simulated from each team's current strength plus its real week-to-week variance; standings are decided by cumulative category win% (this is a Head-to-Head Each Category league), and the top 6 of 12 make the playoffs (Weeks 23-25). Because seeds come from your banked record AND who you still have to play, a team's power rank and its playoff odds can diverge — a strong team that started slow (see Y&Y) can rank high here but still be on the outside, while a banked record (see Burns) can be safe despite cooling off.
methodology_weights: Preseason: DROPPED | W14: 16.7% | W13: 14.2% | W12: 12.1% | W11: 10.3% | W10: 8.7% | W9: 7.4% | W8: 6.3% | W7: 5.4% | W6: 4.6% | W5: 3.9% | W4: 3.3% | W3: 2.8% | W2: 2.4% | W1: 2.0%  ·  Playoff odds: 40k-sim Monte Carlo, top 6 make it, seeds by category win%

rankings.blurb: Takes over the #1 power spot for the first time, and the playoff model agrees it's no fluke: Spoons is the runaway #1 seed (122-65-37) and a 99.7% favorite for a first-round bye. Week 14 was another pitching masterclass — 90 K, 3.22 ERA, 1.01 WHIP, 9 quality starts. They now lead the league in FOUR pitching categories (QS, ERA, WHIP, AND K/BB) and rank 3rd in both strikeouts and steals. The bats cooled in Week 14 (.250, 9 HR) and remain the only soft spot — 9th in AVG, 9th in OBP, dead-last in saves-plus-holds — but the elite run-prevention floor is unmatched in the league. The remaining schedule has some teeth (Burns, Valdez, Caminero, and CLAUDE are all still on the slate), but with a 16-category-game cushion on the #2 seed, none of it threatens the spot. The only suspense left for Spoons is whether they hold the overall #1 seed and its bye — and they're a near-lock to.

Slips to #2 in power after a quiet Week 14 (31 R, 4 HR, .251 — the bats took the week off), but the playoff position is rock-solid: the #2 seed at 115-71-38 and 94.6% for a first-round bye. CLAUDE is still the most balanced roster in the league — 1st in steals (the only 100+ SB team), wins, AND strikeouts, and top-4 in literally every one of the fourteen categories. There is no weakness to attack; even their lowest-ranked cats (QS, SV+H, K/BB) sit 4th-5th. The Week 14 dip was pure variance — the staff still spun a 3.23 ERA — and the seed never wobbled. The one storyline left: a Week 21 date with Spoons could decide the overall #1 seed, but the bats need to wake back up to run them down. A top-2 bye is all but locked either way.

Holds at #3 and is a playoff lock (>99%) as the #3 seed — and Week 14 was the best all-around week of their season: a monster 8 wins, 2.05 ERA, and 5 quality starts alongside 41 runs, a .282 average, and a .367 OBP. The post-trade pitching has fully arrived. Cumulatively Valdez now LEADS the league in RBI (495), OBP (.353), AND saves-plus-holds, and ranks top-3 in runs, homers, K/BB, ERA, and WHIP — a genuinely elite, balanced profile. The lone remaining hole is pitching volume: still last in wins, quality starts, and strikeouts on the season, a scar from the early streaming era — though the 8-win, 5-QS Week 14 shows the current staff is far better than those cumulative ranks suggest. A bye is a long shot (3.9%); Spoons and CLAUDE banked too big a lead. The realistic outcome is the #3 seed and a home matchup in Round 1. The remaining slate is brutal on paper (CLAUDE, Spoons, Burns, ShawSwank all still to come), but the seed is secure enough that these are seeding-and-tune-up games, not survival.

Up to #4 in power and sitting comfortably as the #4 seed (>99% playoff). Week 14 was a power surge — 14 homers, 46 RBI — and cumulatively Caminero now LEADS the league in home runs (155) and ranks 2nd in both RBI and strikeouts. The profile is bats-and-Ks: top-5 in HR, RBI, K, wins, and slugging, but the rate pitching lags (6th-7th in ERA, WHIP, K/BB), which is why the power model holds them at 4th rather than higher. The playoff spot is essentially banked — a 16-game cushion over the bubble — so like the rest of the top five, Caminero is playing for seeding rather than survival. A bye is off the table (1.7%); the #4 seed is both the likely floor and ceiling. The remaining schedule (Spoons, CLAUDE, and the bubble teams) won't change whether they're in.

This is where current strength and playoff position split hard. Y&Y is the model's #5 team — they're genuinely raking (Week 14: .291/.380/.520, 40 R, 13 HR; on the season they LEAD the league in average and rank top-4 in runs and slugging) — but they dug too deep a hole early and sit just 7th in the standings (83-102-39), outside the playoff cut. The model gives them only a 10% playoff shot. The math is unforgiving: they're roughly 10 category-games behind the #6 seed with eight weeks left, and the pitching (bottom-tier in quality starts, strikeouts, and WHIP) caps how fast they can close the gap. The schedule offers no favors — Burns, Valdez, Caminero, and CLAUDE are all still ahead — but their Week 16 head-to-head against Camp, a direct bubble rival, is a genuine swing game. They're the best team on the outside looking in, and they'd need both a heater and some help.

The mirror image of Y&Y directly above: lower current strength, far safer playoff spot. Burns drops to #6 in power after a flat Week 14 (.242, 29 R, 5 HR, 3 QS), but they banked a strong record early and sit pretty as the #5 seed with a >99% playoff lock. Cumulatively they still LEAD the league in runs (541) and rank 2nd in both OBP and average — an elite on-base machine — but the power is simply absent (11th in HR, 10th in RBI), and the recent bats have cooled enough that the model cooled with them. None of that endangers the seed: a 23-category-game cushion over the bubble makes Burns safe regardless of a remaining schedule that opens Spoons-heavy. This is the cleanest illustration of why power rank isn't playoff odds — Burns is the 6th-best team right now and one of the safest bets to play in October.

rankings.team_name: Camp of the New Orleans Saints
rankings.blurb: Camp keeps climbing the power ranks (+2 to #7) on a strong stretch — Week 14: 13 HR, 44 RBI, .281 — and now ranks 4th in both RBI and strikeouts cumulatively. But, like Y&Y, they're behind in the race that counts: 8th in the standings (82-101-41) with a 22% playoff shot. The encouraging news is they're only about 10 category-games back of the #6 seed and trending up; the discouraging news is the rate pitching (9th in ERA AND WHIP) bleeds categories every week. Two games define their season: Week 16 versus Y&Y and Week 18 versus ShawSwank — both direct bubble rivals. Win those head-to-heads and the math shifts in a hurry; lose them and the door swings shut. Very much alive, but they have to take care of business in the games that matter most.

Only the #8 team by current strength — but they're the one holding the final playoff spot. ShawSwank is the #6 seed at exactly .500 (93-93-38), with a 67% shot to stay there: the single live race in the bracket. The specialist build is doing its job — 2nd in wins AND saves-plus-holds, 3rd in quality starts, and a sneaky-strong recent run (Week 14: 1.96 ERA, 1.00 WHIP, 7 QS). The vulnerability is the bats: dead-last in average and 11th in slugging, conceding four hitting categories most weeks, which is why their cushion over the bubble is only ~10 category-games rather than safe. The schedule is a gauntlet (Burns, Valdez, Caminero, and CLAUDE all still to play), and a Week 18 head-to-head with Camp could decide the 6-seed outright. Two-thirds of the time they hold on — but it's the one spot genuinely in play.

Balls ticks up to #9 in power but is effectively out of the race (<1% playoff) at 9th in the standings (85-106-33). Week 14 was a split — 38 R and 96 K, but a brutal 0 saves-plus-holds, which is the structural killer: dead-last in SV+H all season and 11th in steals. They still own one elite trait (2nd in quality starts) and solid rate pitching, but handing the opponent two categories automatically every week (saves and steals) caps the ceiling hard. At roughly 18 category-games back of the cut with eight weeks left, the realistic play is deciding which of their many quality arms to flip to a contender. Mathematically alive; practically, it's evaluation time.

The board's biggest faller — down four to #10 — after a disastrous Week 14 (.193, 20 R, 4 HR, their worst week of the year). PO' BOYD still LEADS the league in slugging and ranks 2nd in homers, but the pitching is a sieve (last in K/BB, 10th in ERA) and the bats cratered at the worst possible time. At 10th in the standings with a <1% playoff shot, the season is functionally over. The all-or-nothing, out-slug-your-own-pitching approach built a fun but doomed roster. The move now is to sell the bats that still carry value and stockpile draft capital for next year's keeper build.

Duran holds at #11 and is out of the playoff hunt (0%) at 11th (74-112-38). The identity hasn't changed all year: pitching volume and speed (near the top in wins, 2nd in steals) propping up a roster that simply can't hit — dead-last in homers, OBP, AND slugging. Week 14 was a small uptick (13 HR, .263), but the offensive floor is far too low to climb a category-win standings. The real asset here is a bullpen stuffed with closers — a genuine trade chip. The smart move is to deal from that relief surplus for next year and play spoiler down the stretch.

Polar stays in the cellar (#12, 0% playoff) at 63-124-37. The post-megatrade roster has been the league's worst all season — last in runs, RBI, steals, AND ERA — with only leftover power (top-7 in HR and slugging) and a quietly strong bullpen (Week 14: 11 saves-plus-holds!) keeping a couple categories respectable. At 12th with no path, it's full rebuild mode: accumulate draft capital, develop the young pieces, and aim at next season. The 2026 race is over for Polar; the 2027 keeper build is officially on.
